تولنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀢𑀮𑀤𑀺 (taladi), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *talati. Compare Punjabi ਤਲਣਾ (talṇā), Gujarati તળવું (taḷvũ), Marathi तळणे (taḷṇe), Odia ତଳିବା (taḷibā).

ex.u: نہانے کے بعد میں دو تولیے استعمال کرتا ہوں۔
ex.r: nahānē kē bād ma͠i dō tauliyē istemāl kartā hū̃.
ex.e: I use two towels after taking a shower.

senses.g: direct plural/ oblique singular/ vocative singular of تَولِیَہ (tauliya, “towel”)

توند.e: Inherited from Prakrit *तोंद (toṃda), तुंद n (tuṃda), from Sanskrit तुन्द n (tunda).

ex.u: اِس گانے کی توہین کیوں کرنا چاہتے ہو؟
ex.r: is gāne ki tauhīn kyū̃ karnā cāhte ho?
ex.e: Why do you want to massacre the song?

توہین.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian توهین (tawhīn), from Arabic تَوْهِين (tawhīn), verbal noun of وَهَّنَ (wahhana, “to debilitate, to dishearten”).

تہ دل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ته دل (tah-i dil). Literally, “bottom of the heart”.

تہمت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian تُهْمَت (tuhmat), borrowed from Arabic تُهْمَة (tuhma). First attested in c. 1682 as Middle Hindi تہمت (thmt /⁠tuhmat⁠/).

تہوار.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi تیوہار (tyvhar /⁠tiyohār⁠/) (c. 1503), from Apabhramsa तिहिवार (tihivāra), from Sanskrit *तिथि-वार (*tithi-vāra).

senses.g: oblique plural of تِہْوَار (tihvār, “festival; celebrate; holiday”)

تیار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian تیار (tayyār), from Arabic طَيَّار (ṭayyār).

ex.u:
چاند پر جو کمند ڈالتے ہیں
مجھ سے کہتے ہیں زندگی بھی تیاگ
cānd par jo kamand ḍālte ha͠i
ex.r: mujh se kahte ha͠i zindgī bhī tyāg
ex.e: (please add an English translation of this quotation)

senses.g: the third day of the lunar fortnight
festival held on the third day of the lunar fortnight of the month of Sawan
Tir, fourth solar month of the Persian calendar

تیر.e: From Sanskrit तीरित (tīrita), from root तरति (tarati).

تیر انداز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian تیرانداز (tīr-andāz), from تیر (tīr, “arrow”) + انداز (andāz, “to throw”).

senses.g: second-person singular possessive pronoun: yours, thine; Early Modern English thine.
second-person singular possessive pronoun, intimate or very polite: thine

تیرا.e: From Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀢𑀏 (tae) + را (rā, genitive affix), the first element of which is from Sanskrit ते (te), variant dative of त्वम् (tvam). Cognate with Classical Persian ترا (turā): تو (tū) + را (rā)

senses.g: second-person singular possessive determiner: your, thy; Early Modern English thy.
second-person singular possessive determiner, intimate or very polite: thy

تیرا.e: From Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀢𑀏 (tae) + را (rā, genitive affix), the first element of which is from Sanskrit ते (te), variant dative of त्वम् (tvam). Cognate with Classical Persian ترا (turā): تو (tū) + را (rā)

senses.g: place of pilgrimage, tirtha; a pilgrimage.
a Tirthankara, one of the 24 teachers of the dharma.

تیرنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀢𑀺𑀭𑀤𑀺 (*tiradi), from Sanskrit तरति (tarati, “crosses over”).

ex.u: یہ قطار تیز ہے۔
ex.e: This queue is fast.

ex.u: تیز چھری
ex.e: sharp knife

ex.u: یہ لڑکی بہت تیز ہے۔
ex.e: This girl is too clever

ex.u: تیز ہوا
ex.e: intense wind

تیزاب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian تیزاب (tēzāb, “aqua fortis”).

تیس.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀢𑀻𑀲 (tīsa), from Sanskrit त्रिंशत् (triṃśat).

تیسرا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit *त्रिःसर (*triḥsara, “three-stringed”), from त्रिः (triḥ, “three”) + सर (sara, “string, cord”).

تیغ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian تیغ (tēġ), possibly either derived from Parthian [script needed] (tyg /⁠tēγ⁠/, “blade; sword, lance”); or from Middle Persian [script needed] (tyh /⁠tēx⁠/, “sharp edge, ridge; ray”).

senses.g: pungent, strong (having a strong, often acidic, odor)

تیکھا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀢𑀺𑀓𑁆𑀔 (tikkha), from Sanskrit तीक्ष्ण (tīkṣṇa, “sharp”), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *(s)teyg-.

ex.u: گاڑی میں تیل ڈَلْوَانا ہَے۔
ex.e: We need to fuel up the car.

تیل.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀢𑁂𑀮 (tela), from Sanskrit तैल (taila). Cognate with Bengali তেল (tel), Gujarati તેલ (tel).

senses.g: tayammum: purifying the hands, face, or other parts of the body by rubbing them with sand or dust, where water is not to be had, for the purpose of purificatory ablution.
a male given name, Taimur or Taimoor, meaning “strong, steel”

تیمور.e: Either through Classical Persian تَیمور (taymur) or directly from Chagatai تیمور (temür, literally “iron”) by way of the Chagatai heritage of the Mughal Empire elite. Ultimately from Proto-Turkic *temür (“iron”).

تین.e: Inherited from Old Hindi तीनि (tīni), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀢𑀺𑀡𑁆𑀡𑀺 (tiṇṇi), from Sanskrit त्रीणि n (trīṇi).

senses.g: A monument in Clifton, Karachi, Sindh, Pakistan consisting of three marble swords to commemorate the three elements of Pakistan’s national motto: Faith, Unity, Discipline
masculine singular imperfect indicative of ہونا (honā)

تھا.e: Inherited from Old Hindi था (thā), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀣𑀺𑀬 (thiya), from Sanskrit स्थित (sthitá), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *stʰHtás, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *stʰHtás, from Proto-Indo-European *sth₂tós. Cognate with Gujarati થયો (thayo) and Latin stātus (whence French été (“been”)).

تھانہ.e: Derived from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀞𑀸𑀡 (ṭhāṇa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit स्थान (sthāna, “place, station”), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *stáHnam, from Proto-Indo-European *stéh₂-no-m, from *steh₂- (“to stand”). Doublet of تھان (thān), سْتھان (sthān), and ـستان (-(i)stān).

senses.g: alternative form of تھائِی لَینْڈ (thāī lainḍ)
to give up; become tired of
to decline; fall (of a market; business etc.)

تھکنا.e: Urdu verb set تَھکْنا (thaknā) تَھکَانا (thakānā) تَھکْوَانا (thakvānā) Etymology tree Proto-Indo-European *steh₂- Sanskrit स्था (sthā) Sanskrit स्थ (stha) Prakrit 𑀣𑀓𑁆𑀓 (thakka)denom. Prakrit 𑀣𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀇 (thakkaï) Apabhramsa थक्कइ (thakkaï) Urdu تھکنا Inherited from Apabhramsa थक्कइ (thakkaï), from Prakrit 𑀣𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀇 (thakkaï), denominal verb from 𑀣𑀓𑁆𑀓 (thakka), from Sanskrit स्थ (stha), from स्था (sthā), from Proto-Indo-European *steh₂-.

senses.g: firm or dry ground; land (as opposed to water)
den, lair (of a lion or tiger)

تھل.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀣𑀮 (thala, “dry place, place”), from Sanskrit स्थल (sthála, “dry land, place”). Cognates include Punjabi تَھࣇ (thaḷ), Nepali थल (thal), Assamese থল (thol), Odia ଥଳ (thaḷa), Old Awadhi थल (thala), Gujarati થળ (thaḷ), Marwari थळ (thaḷ), Marathi थळ (thaḷ).

senses.g: Thanamandi (a town, a tehsil in Rajouri district, Jammu and Kashmir, India)

ex.u: مجھے صرف تھوڑی سی چائے چاہیے۔
ex.e: I just want some tea.

تھوڑا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀣𑁄𑀕 (thoga, “a little”) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀟- (-ḍa-) + -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit स्तोक (stoká, “drop, dot”). Cognate with Punjabi ਥੋੜਾ (thoṛā), Gujarati થોડું (thoḍũ), Marathi थोडे (thoḍe), Bengali থোড়া (thōṛa), Sylheti ꠕꠥꠠꠣ (túṛa).

senses.g: alternative form of تھوک دان (thok dān)

تھوکنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀣𑀼𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀇 (thukkaï), from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀣𑀼𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀢𑀺 (*thukkati). Cognate with Gujarati થૂંકવું (thū̃kvũ), Marathi थुंकणे (thuṅkṇe), Sindhi ٿُڪَڻُ (thukaṇu) / थुकणु (thukaṇu), Punjabi ਥੁੱਕਣਾ (thukkaṇā), Nepali थुक्नु (thuknu), Odia ଥୁକିବା (thukibā). By surface analysis, تُھوک (thūk, “spit; saliva”) + ـنا (-nā, verb-forming suffix).

senses.g: feminine singular imperfect indicative of ہونا (honā)
masculine plural imperfect indicative of ہونا (honā)
The fourth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ت and followed by ث.

ٹالنا.e: Urdu verb set ٹَلْنا (ṭalnā) ٹالْنا (ṭālnā) Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀝𑀸𑀮𑀤𑀺 (ṭāladi, “removes, frightens away”), from Sanskrit टालयति (ṭālayati, “cause to move”).

ٹانگ.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀝𑀁𑀓 (ṭaṃka), from Sanskrit टङ्क (ṭaṅka). First attested as Middle Hindi ٿانگ (ṭang /⁠ṭang⁠/) / ٿنگ (ṭng /⁠ṭng⁠/).

senses.g: Tangail District: One of the thirteen districts in the Dhaka Division of Bangladesh
tanka (a copper coin worth two paisa)

ٹکا.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi ٿکا (ṭka /⁠ṭakā⁠/) (c. 1639), derived from Sanskrit टङ्क (ṭaṅka). Sense 1 is a semantic loan from Bengali টাকা (ṭaka). Cognate with Gujarati ટકો (ṭako).

senses.g: collision, hit, crash (impact between two objects)

ٹکر.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀝𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀭 (ṭakkara). Cognate with Punjabi ਟੱਕਰ (ṭakkar) / ٹَکَّر (ṭakkar), Gujarati ટક્કર (ṭakkar), Marathi टक्कर (ṭakkar), Bengali টক্কর (ṭokkor).

ٹکڑا.e: Inherited from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *𑀝𑀼𑀓𑁆𑀓 (*ṭukka, “piece”) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀟- (-ḍa-) + -𑀓- (-ka-), from *टुक्क् (ṭukk, “to cut, break”), from Proto-Middle Indo-Aryan *𑀢𑁆𑀭𑀼𑀓𑁆𑀓𑁆 (*trukk). Cognate with Assamese টুকুৰা (tukura), Bengali টুকরা (ṭukra).

senses.g: Tilford (a village and civil parish south-east of Farnham, Waverley borough, Surrey, England (OS grid ref SU8743))

ex.u: موجودہ نو تعمیر شدہ مرکز احمدیت اسلام آباد کا اولین ریکارڈ 1762ء کے نقشہ جات سے ملتا ہے۔ دریائے وے کے دونوں اطراف میں ٹلفورڈ برج نامی گاؤں کا ذکر ملتا ہے جو کہ اب ٹلفورڈ گرین کے نام سے جانی جاتی ہے۔
ex.r: maujūdah nau taʻmīr śiddah markaz-e aḥmadiyyat islām ābād kā āvvalīn rīkārḍ 1762ʻ ke naqśah jāt se miltā hai. dariya'e ve ke donoṉ at̤rāf meṉ ṭilforḍ brij nāmī gā'oṉ kā ẕikr miltā hai jo kih ab ṭilforḍ grīn ke nām se jānī jātī hai.
ex.e: The earliest record of the newly inaugurated headquarters of Ahmadiyyat, Islamabad, can be found in maps dated 1762 AD. On both sides of the River Way there is mention of a village named Tylford Bridge, now known as Tilford Green.

ٹلفورڈ.e: Borrowed from English Tilford or Tylford.

ٹماٹر.e: Borrowed from English tomato, from Spanish tomate, from Classical Nahuatl tomatl.

senses.g: Tando Allahyar (a city in Tando Allahyar district, Sindh, Pakistan)

ٹنڈو الہ یار.e: From Sindhi ٽنڊوالهيار (ṭanḍōalahyār), named after Mir Allahyar Talpur + Baluchi ٹنڈو (ṭanḍō) (cantonment).

senses.g: Tando Adam (a city in Sanghar district, Sindh, Pakistan)

ٹنڈو آدم.e: From Sindhi ٽنڊو آدم (ṭanḍō ādam), named after Mir Adam Khan Mari + Baluchi ٹنڈو (ṭanḍō) (cantonment).

senses.g: Tando Muhammad Khan (a city in Tando Muhammad Khan district, Sindh, Pakistan)

ٹنڈو محمد خان.e: From Sindhi ٽنڊو محمد خان (ṭanḍō muhammad xān), named after Mir Muhammad Khan Talpur Shahwani + Baluchi ٹنڈو (ṭanḍō) (cantonment).

ex.u: ٹوپی بَدَلْنا
ex.e: to crown a new king

ٹوپی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀝𑁄𑀧𑀺𑀆 (ṭopiā, “helmet”) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-), related to Sanskrit टोपिका (ṭopikā).

ٹوٹنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀝𑀼𑀝𑁆𑀝𑀇 (ṭuṭṭaï, “to break”), ultimately from Sanskrit तृद् (tṛd, “to cleave, split”). Cognate to Punjabi ਟੁੱਟਣਾ (ṭuṭṭaṇā), Gujarati તૂટવું (tūṭvũ), Marathi तुटणे (tuṭṇe), Bengali টুটা (ṭuṭa), Odia ତୁଟିବା (tuṭibā).

ٹوٹی.e: Borrowed from Punjabi ٹُوٹی (ṭūṭī). Doublet of ٹوٹی (ṭoṭī).

senses.g: inflection of ٹُوٹا (ṭūṭā): — direct masculine plural
inflection of ٹُوٹا (ṭūṭā): — oblique masculine singular/plural

ٹوکری.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀝𑁄𑀓𑁆𑀓 (*ṭokka). Cognate to Dhivehi ޓުކުރި (ṭukuri), Bengali টোকা (ṭōka), Sindhi ٽوڪِرو (ṭokiro), Punjabi ਟੋਕਰਾ (ṭokrā), and Nepali टोकरा (ṭokarā).

ٹیڑھا.e: Inherited from Old Hindi टेढौ (ṭeḍhau), from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *𑀢𑁆𑀭𑁂𑀟𑁆𑀟 (*treḍḍa).

senses.g: Taxila (a city in Rawalpindi district, Punjab, Pakistan)

ٹیکسلا.e: Borrowed from English Taxila, from Ancient Greek Τάξιλα (Táxila), from Sanskrit तक्षशिला (takṣaśilā, “City of Cut Stone”), a compound of तक्ष (takṣa) + शिला (śilā́, “stone”).

ex.u: وہ ٹیلی وِیژَن کی مَشْہُور تَرِین ہَسْتِیوں میں سے ہَے۔
ex.r: vo ṭelī-vīźan kī maśhūr-tarīn hastiyõ mẽ se hai.
ex.e: He is among television's most famous celebrities.

ٹھڈی.e: Inherited from Prakrit *ठुड्ड (ṭhuḍḍa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-) without cluster simplification. See Prakrit 𑀢𑀼𑀁𑀟 (tuṃḍa) for more. Compare Hindi ठोड़ी (ṭhoṛī), टोटी (ṭoṭī), टोंटी (ṭõṭī).

ٹھکانا.e: Related to ٹھیک (ṭhīk). Cognate with Punjabi ਟਿਕਾਣਾ (ṭikāṇā) / ٹِکانا (ṭikānā), Gujarati ઠેકાણું (ṭhekāṇũ), Marathi ठिकाण (ṭhikāṇ), Bengali ঠিকানা (ṭhikana).

ٹھگ.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀞𑀕 (ṭhaga), of uncertain origin. See there for more.

ٹھنڈا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀣𑀟𑁆𑀠 (thaḍḍha), 𑀣𑀤𑁆𑀥 (thaddha, “fixed, firm”), from Sanskrit स्तब्ध (stabdha).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀣𑀟𑁆𑀠 (thaḍḍha), 𑀣𑀤𑁆𑀥 (thaddha, “fixed, firm”), from Sanskrit स्तब्ध (stabdha).

ٹھنگنا.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀞𑀺𑀁𑀕 (*ṭhiṃga). Compare Punjabi ਠਿਗਣਾ (ṭhigṇā) / ٹھِگݨا (ṭhigṇā), Gujarati ઠીંગણું (ṭhī̃gṇũ), Marathi ठेंगणे (ṭheṅgṇe).

ٹھوڑی.e: Inherited from Prakrit *𑀞𑁄𑀟𑁆𑀟 (*ṭhŏḍḍa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-). See 𑀢𑀼𑀁𑀟 (tuṃḍa) for more.

ex.u: حکومت نے ٹھوس پیشرفت پایا ہے
ex.r: hukūmat ne ṭhos peśraft pāyā hai
ex.e: the government has made substantial progress

ٹھہرنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit *𑀞𑀳𑀺𑀭𑀇 (*ṭhahiraï), denominal verb from Sanskrit *स्त॒भि॒र (*stabhirá), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *stabʰrás, from Proto-Indo-European *stebʰ-ró-s, from *stebʰ- + *-rós.

ex.u: اَپْنی غَلْطِیاں جَھٹ سے ٹِھیک کَرْنا چَاہِئے۔
ex.r: apnī ġaltiyā̃ jhaṭ se ṭhīk karnā cāhie.
ex.e: You should quickly correct your mistakes.

ex.u: ہاں مَیں ٹِھیک ہُوں۔
ex.e: Yes, I'm fine.

senses.g: immediate; very close, direct or adjacent

ex.u: شراب کا ٹھیکا
ex.e: alcohol shop

senses.g: The fourth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ٹ and followed by ج.

ثابت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ثابت (sābit), from Arabic ثَابِت (ṯābit), active participle of ثَبَتَ (ṯabata, “to be firm, to be fixed, to persist”).

ثانیہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ثانیه (sāniya), from Arabic ثَانِيَة (ṯāniya).

ثبوت.e: Borrowed from Arabic ثُبُوت (ṯubūt), possibly via Classical Persian.

ثروت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ثَرْوَت (sarwat), from Arabic ثَرْوَة (ṯarwa).

senses.g: a female given name, Surayya, from Arabic

ثریا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ثُرَیّا, from Arabic ثُرَيَّا (ṯurayyā). First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi ثریا (s̱rya /⁠surayyā⁠/).

senses.g: the skies (as opposed to the ground)

ثریا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ثُرَیّا, from Arabic ثُرَيَّا (ṯurayyā). First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi ثریا (s̱rya /⁠surayyā⁠/).

ثقالت.e: From Classical Persian ثقالت (saqālat), from Arabic verbal noun ثَقَالَة (ṯaqāla), from verb root ثَقُلَ (ṯaqula, “to be heavy”).

ثواب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ثواب (sawāb), from Arabic ثَوَاب (ṯawāb).

senses.g: The seventh letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ث and followed by چ.

جابجا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جابجا (jā-ba-jā, literally “(from) place to place”).

جادو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَادُو (jādū), from Middle Persian 𐭩𐭠𐭲𐭥𐭪𐭠 (yʾtʿkʾ /⁠ǰādūg⁠/, “wizardry; wizard”), from Old Persian 𐎹𐎠𐎬𐎢 (y-a-tu-u /⁠yātu⁠/), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *yaHtúš.

ex.u: کوئی جادوگر کے بازو کاٹ بھی دے اس کے ہاتھ میں پھر بھی جادو رہتا ہے۔
ex.r: koī jādūgar ke bāzū kāṭ bhī de us-ke hāth mẽ phir bhī jādū rahtā hai.
ex.e: Even if someone cuts off a magician's arms the magic still remains in his hands.

senses.g: Georgia (a transcontinental country in the Caucasus, spanning both Europe and Asia; capital: Tbilisi)
Georgia (a state of the United States)

جاری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جاری, from Arabic جَارِي (jārī).

ex.u:
دل میں خیالِ یار ہے جاسوس کی نمط
رکھتا ہوں اس کوں ننگ سوں ناموس کی نمط
dil mẽ xayāl-e-yār hai jāsūs kī namat
ex.r: rakhtā hū̃ is kū̃ naṅg sū̃ nāmūs kī namat
ex.e: (please add an English translation of this quotation)

جاسوس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جاسوس (jāsūs), from Arabic جَاسُوس (jāsūs), from Classical Syriac ܓܫܘܫܐ (gāšōšā).

senses.g: inflection of جاگْنا (jāgnā): — second-person singular intimate present imperative
verbal noun of جاگْنا (jāgnā, “to wake up”)

جال.e: Inherited from Sanskrit जाल (jāla). Cognate with Assamese জাল (zal), Punjabi ਜਾਲ਼ (jāḷ), Sinhalese දැල (dæla).

senses.g: night watch (for a period of three hours)

جامن.e: Inherited from Sanskrit *जम्बून (*jambūna), a by-form of जम्बुल (jambula).

جانا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀚𑀸𑀇 (jāi), from Ashokan Prakrit 𑀬𑀸𑀢𑀺 (yāti), from Sanskrit याति॑ (yā́ti), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *yáHti, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *yáHti, from Proto-Indo-European *yéh₂ti, from *yeh₂-.

جانب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جانِب, from Arabic جَانِب (jānib).

جانچنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀚𑀸𑀘𑁆𑀘𑀤𑀺 (*jāccadi), *𑀚𑀘𑁆𑀘𑀤𑀺 (*jaccadi), from Sanskrit याच्यते (yācyate, “to be asked”). Cognate with Nepali जाँच्नु (jā̃cnu).

جاننا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀚𑀸𑀡 (jāṇa) + ـنا (-nā, verbal suffix), the first element of which is from Sanskrit ज्ञा (jñā). Cognate with Marathi जाणणे (jāṇṇe), Old English cnāwan (whence English know).

senses.g: inflection of جانْنا (jānnā, “to know”): — second-person singular familiar future subjunctive
inflection of جانْنا (jānnā, “to know”): — second-person singular familiar imperative
first-person singular future subjunctive of جانْنا (jānnā, “to know; comprehend”)

جاوتری.e: Borrowed from Gujarati જાવંત્રી (jāvantrī) or Northwestern Indo-Aryan.

جاوید.e: From Classical Persian جاوید (jāwēd, “eternal”), from Middle Persian [script needed] (yʾwyt' /⁠jāwēd⁠/, “eternal”).

senses.g: a male given name, Javed, Jawed, or Jaweed, from Persian

جاوید.e: From Classical Persian جاوید (jāwēd, “eternal”), from Middle Persian [script needed] (yʾwyt' /⁠jāwēd⁠/, “eternal”).

جاہل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جاهل (jāhil), from Arabic جَاهِل (jāhil).

ex.u: ذیشان نے بہت ساری جائدادیں بنائی ہیں۔
ex.r: zīśān ne bahut sārī jāidādẽ banā'ī ha͠i.
ex.e: Zeeshaan has accumulated a lot wealth.

جائداد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَای دَاد (jāy dād, “place (that was) made”), from جَای (jāy) + دَادَن (dādan). Cognate with Punjabi جَداد (jadād).

جائزہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جائزه (jā'iza, “prize; examination; confirmation”), from Arabic جَائِزَة (jāʔiza, “prize, reward”).

جب.e: From اب (ab, “now”). Cognate with Punjabi ਜਦੋਂ (jadõ) / جدوں (jadõ).

senses.g: Gibraltar (a peninsula, city, and overseas territory of the United Kingdom, at the southern end of Iberia)
Gibraltar, Strait of Gibraltar (a strait connecting the Mediterranean to the Atlantic between Gibraltar and Morocco; in full, Strait of Gibraltar)

جبرالٹر.e: Borrowed from English Gibraltar, taken from Spanish Gibraltar, itself from Arabic جَبَل طَارِق (jabal ṭāriq), meaning "Mountain of Tariq".

senses.g: Gabriel (an archangel associated with carrying messages from God.)

جبرائیل.e: Borrowed from Arabic جِبْرَائِيل (jibrāʔīl), ultimately from Hebrew גַּבְרִיאֵל (gavri'él, gaḇrīʾēl). Doublet of جبریل (jibrīl).

جبڑا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit जम्भ (jámbha) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀟- (-ḍa-) + -𑀓- (-ka-), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *ȷ́ámbʰas, from Proto-Indo-European *ǵómbʰos. Cognate with Marathi जबडा (jabḍā).

senses.g: Jabalpur (a city in Madhya Pradesh, India)
Byblos (a port city in Keserwan-Jbeil Governorate, Lebanon)

جبیل.e: Borrowed from Arabic جُبَيْل (jubayl), from Phoenician 𐤂𐤁𐤋 (gbl).

senses.g: Jatoi (a city in Muzaffargarh district, Punjab, Pakistan)
the hair of Shiva, which is matted into dreadlocks, like the hair of a sadhu

جٹھانی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀚𑀺𑀝𑁆𑀞𑀸𑀡𑀻 (jiṭṭhāṇī) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-), from Sanskrit ज्येष्ठ-जानि (jyeṣṭha-jāni).

senses.g: direct plural of جِٹھانی (jiṭhānī, “husband's elder brother's wife”)
vocative plural of جِٹھانی (jiṭhānī, “husband's elder brother's wife”)
oblique plural of جِٹھانی (jiṭhānī, “husband's elder brother's wife”)

جثہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جثه (jussa), from Arabic جُثَّة (juṯṯa).

جدا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جدا (judā, “separate, apart”). Compare Punjabi جُدا (judā) / ਜੁਦਾ (judā), Gujarati જુદું (judũ).

جدائی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جُدَایِی (judāyī), from جُدَا (judā /jodâ, “separate”) + ـیِی (-yī /-yi).

جدید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جدید (jadīd), from Arabic جَدِيد (jadīd).

جذب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَذْب (jazb), from Arabic جَذْب (jaḏb).

جذبہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جذبه, from Arabic جَذْب (jaḏb).

جرأت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جُرْأَت (jur'at), from Arabic جُرْأَة (jurʔa). First attested in c. 1741 as Middle Hindi جرات (jrat /⁠jurat⁠/).

جراح.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جراح (jarrāh), from Arabic جَرَّاح (jarrāḥ).

جراحت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جِرَاحَت (jirāhat), from Arabic جِرَاحَة (jirāḥa).

جرثومہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جرثومه (jursūma), from Arabic جرثومة (jurṯūma, “germ, microbe”).

جرم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جُرْم (jurm), from Arabic جُرْم (jurm).

جرمانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جرمانه (jurmāna). By surface analysis, جُرْم (jurm, “crime”) + انَہ (āna).

senses.g: inflection of جُرْمانَہ (jurmāna, “fine; penalty”): — direct plural
inflection of جُرْمانَہ (jurmāna, “fine; penalty”): — oblique/vocative singular
Jaranwala (a city in Faisalabad district, Punjab, Pakistan)

ex.u: جُڑْواں بَچّے
ex.e: twins

senses.g: twin (either of two people who shared the same uterus, or of two similar or closely related objects)

جزاک اللہ.e: Borrowed from Arabic جَزَاكَ ٱللَّٰهُ (jazāka llāhu).

senses.g: Andaman and Nicobar Islands (a union territory of India; capital: Port Blair)

جزائر انڈمان و نکوبار.e: Calque of Hindi अंडमान और निकोबार द्वीपसमूह (aṇḍamān aur nikobār dvīpsamūh)

جزیرہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَزِیرَه (jazīra), borrowed from Arabic جَزِيرَة (jazīra).

senses.g: bite the hand that feeds one

جس ہانڈی میں کھانا اسی میں چھید کرنا.c: جِس ہانْڈی میں کَھانا اُسی میں چھید کَرْنا
جس ہانڈی میں کھانا اسی میں چھید کرنا.r: jis hānḍī mẽ khānā usī mẽ ched karnā
جس ہانڈی میں کھانا اسی میں چھید کرنا.h: जिस हांडी में खाना उसी में छेद करना
جس ہانڈی میں کھانا اسی میں چھید کرنا.ipa: /d͡ʒɪs ɦɑːn.ɖiː mẽː kʰɑː.nɑː ʊ.siː mẽː t͡ʃʰeːd̪ kəɾ.nɑː/
جس ہانڈی میں کھانا اسی میں چھید کرنا.e: Literally, “to puncture the cooking pot from which one eats from”.

جسامت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جَسامَت, from Arabic جَسَامَة (jasāma).

جستجو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جستجو (justujō), with the majhūl vowel shifted to a ma'rūf vowel (ō > ū).

جسم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جِسْم (jism), from Arabic جِسْم (jism).

جسور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian جسور (jasūr), from Arabic جَسُور (jasūr).
